title: Tbilisi, Georgia: The Ultimate Digital Nomad Guide (2025)

interlinking_suggestions.title: Georgia's 'Remotely from Georgia' Program: A Simple Visa Path

The 1% Tax Regime for Entrepreneurs in Georgia

author.name: Isabelle Rossi
author.bio: Having lived and worked in over 30 countries, Isabelle is a leading voice on the digital nomad movement. She covers everything from visa strategies and travel hacking to maintaining well-being on the road.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: To secure Georgia's 1% tax rate, you must first register as an Individual Entrepreneur and then immediately apply for 'Small Business Status' via the Revenue Service portal.
Maintain compliance by filing a tax declaration by the 15th of every month for the previous month's income, even if your turnover was zero, to avoid penalties.

For US citizens, proactively manage dual tax obligations by leveraging the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) and filing the mandatory Foreign Bank Account Report (FBAR) to avoid severe penalties.
Establish a dedicated business bank account immediately after IE registration to create a clean, auditable trail of all revenue, which is your primary defense against compliance questions.
summary: For global professionals, the primary challenge of operating from Tbilisi is mitigating the compliance and bureaucratic risks that threaten their business autonomy. This playbook provides a direct solution: a systematic guide to registering as an Individual Entrepreneur, securing the 1% tax status, and maintaining rigorous monthly financial reporting. By following this structured plan, readers can build a resilient, audit-proof operational base that transforms Georgia’s powerful benefits into a secure platform for capital growth and efficiency.
ai_overview_answer:
Establishing a business base in Tbilisi, Georgia requires a structured approach focused on legal registration and tax optimization. The core process involves registering as an Individual Entrepreneur (IE) at a Public Service Hall and then applying for "Small Business Status" online to access the 1% tax rate on annual turnover up to GEL 500,000. Key initial steps include obtaining a Georgian Tax Identification Number (TIN) and opening a local bank account. Once established, your primary ongoing duty is to file a monthly tax declaration by the 15th of each month, even if your income is zero.

For U.S. citizens, it is crucial to manage dual obligations by leveraging the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) and filing the mandatory Foreign Bank Account Report (FBAR). This formal process is separate from Georgia's generous one-year visa-free stay, which grants the right to reside but not to work without proper business registration. By combining this streamlined setup with a low cost of living, professionals can create a highly efficient and compliant operational base. This strategic approach transforms Tbilisi from a travel destination into a powerful platform for capital growth and business efficiency.

@graph.name: How to Set Up a Business Base in Tbilisi, Georgia
@graph.description: A step-by-step guide for global professionals to establish a compliant and tax-optimized business base in Tbilisi, Georgia, leveraging the 1% tax regime.

step.name: Obtain Your Tax Identification Number (TIN)
step.text: Your first stop is a local Public Service Hall or Revenue Service office. The process is remarkably efficient and free. Present your passport and complete a short application. Within an hour, you will be issued a nine-digit TIN, the master key to every subsequent step.

step.name: Select Your Banking Partner
step.text: Your bank is a core piece of your financial infrastructure. Both TBC Bank and Bank of Georgia are listed on the London Stock Exchange, ensuring transparency. You should leverage their premium services, which cater to international professionals.

step.name: Establish Your Physical Base
step.text: Secure a furnished, work-ready apartment for 1-3 months in a professional-friendly neighborhood like Vake or Vera. Simultaneously, ensure redundant connectivity. Acquire a local SIM card from Magti or Silknet and arrange for high-speed fiber internet at your apartment.

step.name: Register as an Individual Entrepreneur (IE) with 1% Tax Status
step.text: This is a two-step process. First, register as an IE at a Public Service Hall with your passport and a legal Georgian address. Second, immediately after your IE registration is complete, apply for Small Business Status (SBS) through the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge). This is the crucial step that changes your tax rate to 1% on total turnover.

step.name: Internalize the Rules
step.text: The 1% tax regime is conditional. The 1% rate applies to an annual turnover up to GEL 500,000. Income exceeding this cap is taxed at 3%. The status is designed for service providers and excludes certain activities. Verify your business activities are eligible.

step.name: Open Your Business Bank Account
step.text: Operating without a dedicated business account is a strategic error. It ensures clean bookkeeping and creates an auditable trail of all business income. With your IE certificate in hand, opening a multi-currency business account at your chosen bank is a straightforward final step.

step.name: Master Monthly Tax Declarations
step.text: Your core compliance task is filing a monthly tax declaration by the 15th of each month for the preceding month's income. This is a strict, non-negotiable deadline managed through the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge). You must file a declaration every single month, even if your income was zero.

step.name: Synchronize Dual Obligations (for US Expats)
step.text: For American professionals, mastering Georgian compliance is only half the battle. The US taxes its citizens on worldwide income. Your strategy will revolve around three core concepts: Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE), Foreign Bank Account Report (FBAR), and Foreign Tax Credit (FTC).

mainEntity.name: How do I register for Georgia's 1% tax for freelancers?

acceptedAnswer.text: The process is sequential. First, register as an **Individual Entrepreneur (IE)** at a Public Service Hall. Once complete, apply for **"Small Business Status"** through the Georgian Revenue Service's online portal (rs.ge). This status grants the 1% tax rate and becomes effective from the first day of the month *following* your application.

mainEntity.name: What are the requirements for the Georgia Individual Entrepreneur program?

acceptedAnswer.text: The core requirements are: be physically present to register (or use a Power of Attorney), provide your passport, have a legal address in Georgia with owner consent, maintain an annual turnover under GEL 500,000, and engage in eligible business activities (most service-based professions like IT and marketing are eligible; legal, medical, and audit consulting are not).

mainEntity.name: How do I open a business bank account in Georgia as a foreigner?

acceptedAnswer.text: After you are registered as an Individual Entrepreneur, you can open a business account. While not legally required, it is essential for clean bookkeeping. Visit a bank like TBC or Bank of Georgia and present your passport and your IE registration certificate, which contains your Tax Identification Number (TIN).

mainEntity.name: Does Georgia's 1-year visa-free stay allow you to work?

acceptedAnswer.text: This is a critical distinction. The one-year visa-free policy grants you the legal right to *reside* in Georgia. To *work compliantly* as a self-employed professional and benefit from the tax system, you must formalize your activity by registering as an Individual Entrepreneur. The visa provides legal presence; the IE registration provides the legal framework for business operations.

mainEntity.name: Is Tbilisi a good base for a US expat professional?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, for the strategic professional, Tbilisi offers a powerful combination of a low cost of living, minimal tax burden, and removal of bureaucratic hurdles. However, you remain obligated to file US taxes. Proactively managing this by leveraging tools like the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) and filing necessary reports like the FBAR is non-negotiable.

mainEntity.name: What are the ongoing reporting obligations for an Individual Entrepreneur in Georgia?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your primary duty is to file a monthly tax declaration via the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge) by the 15th of each month, reporting the previous month's gross turnover. The 1% tax is due at the same time. You must file every month, even if you have zero income.

mainEntity.name: How does the 1% tax in Georgia interact with US tax obligations like FBAR?

acceptedAnswer.text: These are two separate compliance domains. The **1% Tax** is your obligation to Georgia based on your business turnover. **FBAR** is a *reporting* requirement to the U.S. Treasury if your foreign financial accounts exceed $10,000. Paying your Georgian tax does not exempt you from US filing obligations. You must manage both systems in parallel, typically using the FEIE or Foreign Tax Credit to avoid double taxation on your US return.

body.content: Tbilisi, Georgia: The 2025 Operational Playbook for Your Global Business

You didn’t build a borderless career by following generic advice. You are a "Business-of-One," a high-performing professional operating on a global scale, and your primary concern isn’t finding the trendiest cafe—it’s mitigating risk. The very idea of misinterpreting tax law or stumbling into a bureaucratic trap in a foreign country represents a direct threat to the autonomy you’ve worked so hard to create. This is where most conversations about Tbilisi, Georgia, fall short. They sell a romantic dream of wine and mountains but gloss over the operational realities.
This is not another digital nomad guide.

Consider this your strategic playbook, designed to directly address your core anxieties: **compliance, risk, and control**. We will move beyond surface-level attractions to provide a detailed, phased approach for establishing a resilient and profitable operational base in Georgia. The incredible benefits, like the 1% tax regime for an Individual Entrepreneur and one of the world's most generous visa-free policies, are powerful tools. But tools are only effective when you have a clear blueprint. This guide provides that blueprint, systematically walking you through the legal, financial, and logistical steps required to build a compliant, audit-proof foundation for your business. Let's begin.
Phase 1: Your 30-Day Strategic Setup Sprint

The moment you land in a new country, you face a deluge of critical tasks that can quickly erode your focus. We will counter that chaos with structure. Forget aimless wandering; view your first 30 days as a highly focused project—a **Strategic Setup Sprint** designed to build your complete operational foundation. The objective is to systematically move you from an individual with a passport to a legally recognized, tax-optimized, and financially operational "Business-of-One."
This sprint condenses months of potential confusion into a managed project, front-loading the administrative work so you can achieve operational readiness with speed and precision.
Weeks 1-2: Infrastructure & Integration
Your first two weeks are dedicated to building the core pillars of your new base. This is about creating a stable operational runway.

items: **Mission 1: Obtain Your Tax Identification Number (TIN).** Before anything else, you must exist within Georgia's tax system. Your first stop is a local **Public Service Hall** or **Revenue Service** office. The process is remarkably efficient and free. Present your passport and complete a short application (staff are accustomed to assisting foreigners). Within an hour, you will be issued a nine-digit TIN, the master key to every subsequent step.

**Mission 2: Select Your Banking Partner.** Your bank is a core piece of your financial infrastructure. Both TBC Bank and Bank of Georgia are listed on the London Stock Exchange, ensuring transparency. You should leverage their premium services, which cater to international professionals.

**Mission 3: Establish Your Physical Base.** Resist the urge to sign a long-term lease immediately. Secure a furnished, work-ready apartment for 1-3 months in a professional-friendly neighborhood like **Vake or Vera**. This provides a stable environment to execute the rest of your sprint. Simultaneously, ensure redundant connectivity. Acquire a local SIM card from **Magti** (best coverage) or **Silknet** (strong English support) and arrange for high-speed fiber internet at your apartment. A robust mobile data plan as a failover for your primary connection ensures you are never offline.

rows: **Core Offering**
Premium packages with a focus on personalized advisory and lifestyle benefits.
A high-end service bundling a personal banker, travel perks, and preferential rates.
**English Support**
Strong, with dedicated advisors for premium clients reachable by phone or email.
Well-regarded, with personal bankers assigned to Solo clients to assist remotely.
**Digital Experience**
Modern, sleek mobile app and online platform often praised for its user interface.
Comprehensive digital services allowing for most transactions without visiting a branch.

body.content: Weeks 3-4: Formalization & Optimization
With foundational elements in place, you will now formalize your business structure to unlock Georgia's primary financial benefits.

items: **Mission 4: Register as an Individual Entrepreneur (IE) with 1% Tax Status.** This is a two-step process, often completed in a single day.
**Mission 5: Internalize the Rules.** The 1% tax regime is powerful but conditional.

**Mission 6: Open Your Business Bank Account.** While not legally mandated, operating without a dedicated business account is a strategic error. It ensures clean bookkeeping and creates an auditable trail of all business income—your primary defense against compliance questions. With your IE certificate in hand, opening a multi-currency business account at your chosen bank is a straightforward final step.

**Register as an IE:** Visit a **Public Service Hall** with your passport and a legal Georgian address. The property owner must provide consent, either in person or via a notarized document. Registration is typically confirmed within one business day.

**Apply for Small Business Status (SBS):** Immediately after your IE registration is complete, apply for SBS through the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge). This is the crucial step that changes your tax rate from the standard 20% on profit to **1% on total turnover**. The 1% rate applies from the month *after* your application is approved, so timing is critical.

**Revenue Cap:** The 1% rate applies to an annual turnover up to **GEL 500,000** (approx. $185,000 USD). Income exceeding this cap is taxed at 3%. If your turnover exceeds GEL 500,000 for two consecutive years, your SBS will be revoked.

**Disqualified Activities:** The status is designed for service providers (e.g., IT, marketing, consulting). It excludes most legal, medical, architectural, and audit/tax consulting services, among others. Verify your business activities are eligible.

body.content: Phase 2: Mastering Ongoing Compliance

With your business established, the focus shifts from setup to professional, long-term operation. This phase is about building a fortress of compliance around your IE status, transforming it from a tax advantage into a resilient, predictable operational base.
The Monthly Rhythm: Georgian Tax Declarations

Your core compliance task is filing a monthly tax declaration by the **15th of each month** for the preceding month's income. This is a strict, non-negotiable deadline managed through the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge).

items: **Invoicing for Clarity:** Your invoices are your primary evidence. Each must contain your full name and IE registration number, legal address, client details, a unique invoice number, a clear description of services, and the total amount due with currency specified.

**Handling Nuances:** The 1% tax applies to your **gross turnover**. You cannot deduct business expenses. If a client reimburses you for a cost, that reimbursement is considered revenue and is subject to the 1% tax. Structure your service fees accordingly.

**The "Zero" Declaration:** You must file a declaration **every single month**, even if your income was zero. Filing a "zero" declaration is a crucial signal that you are an active, compliant business operator. Forgetting to do so is a common and easily avoidable error that can lead to penalties.

body.content: The US Expat Alert: Synchronizing Dual Obligations

For American professionals, mastering Georgian compliance is only half the battle. The US taxes its citizens on worldwide income, requiring a parallel layer of financial discipline. Synchronizing your obligations to both countries is fundamental to long-term risk management. Your strategy will revolve around three core concepts.

header: Key Consideration for Your Georgian Business
rows: **Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE)**
An IRS provision allowing you to exclude a significant portion of foreign-earned income from US federal tax (projected up to $130,000 for 2025).
To qualify, you must meet the "Physical Presence Test" (330 days outside the US in a 12-month period) or the "Bona Fide Residence Test." Your IE income is eligible.
**Foreign Bank Account Report (FBAR)**
A mandatory annual disclosure to the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) if your combined foreign bank accounts exceed $10,000 at any point during the year.
This is a reporting requirement, not a tax. Failure to file Form 114 (the FBAR) carries severe penalties, making this a critical, non-negotiable annual task.
**Foreign Tax Credit (FTC)**
A dollar-for-dollar credit for income taxes paid to a foreign government, used to offset your US tax liability on the same income.
This is relevant for income exceeding the FEIE limit. While your 1% Georgian tax is low, the FTC can still be used to offset US tax on that higher tranche of income.

body.content: The burden of proof rests on you. Maintain an "audit-proof" digital shoebox with every Georgian tax declaration, all bank statements, and a clear travel log with flight records to substantiate your FEIE claim.
Asset Protection: The Georgian Banking System

Placing operational capital in a foreign country requires confidence in its financial stability. The Georgian banking sector is stable and well-regulated, with the National Bank of Georgia commended for its prudent policies. For depositors, this is backstopped by a formal government guarantee: the Deposit Insurance System insures funds up to **30,000 GEL** per depositor, per bank. For professionals holding significant revenue, the standard risk mitigation strategy applies: spread capital across multiple licensed banks to maximize coverage.
Phase 3: Leveraging Your Base for Growth & Lifestyle

With your compliance fortress built, you can shift from defense to offense. This is where you transform your resilient base into a powerful engine for growth and an exceptional quality of life, leveraging Tbilisi's unique advantages to fuel your professional ambitions.
The Lifestyle Dividend: A High-Performance Budget

The low cost of living in Tbilisi is not about backpacker budgeting; it’s about strategically allocating capital to buy back time and eliminate stress. For a professional with a monthly turnover of $10,000, after the 1% tax, approximately $9,900 is available to engineer a life optimized for deep work.

rows: **Housing**
Premium, renovated 1-2 bedroom apartment in a desirable, quiet neighborhood like Vake or Vera.
**Professional Infrastructure**
Private office at a top co-working space (e.g., Terminal) and a premium gym membership.
**Health & Wellness**
Comprehensive private health insurance plus out-of-pocket wellness expenses.
**Domestic Help**
Part-time cleaner for 4-6 hours per week, handling all domestic chores.
**Groceries & Dining**
High-quality groceries and regular dining at Tbilisi's best restaurants.
**Transportation**
Primarily ride-sharing services (Bolt) for efficiency.
**Utilities & Internet**
High-speed fiber optic internet and all associated utilities.
**Total Estimated Spend**
**Remaining Capital**
*For reinvestment, savings, or strategic travel.*

body.content: This demonstrates the core thesis: a high-performance lifestyle consumes only a fraction of your income, freeing up thousands per month for wealth generation.
Building Your Professional Ecosystem
Your success hinges on an infrastructure that mirrors the quality of your work.

items: **Vetting Co-working Spaces:** The primary criteria are not free coffee but **acoustic privacy, internet redundancy, and professional meeting rooms**. **Terminal**, with multiple locations, is the leader for focused operators, offering private, enclosed offices ideal for deep work and confidential client calls.

**Networking for CEOs, Not Backpackers:** Move beyond casual expat meetups. Engage with organizations like the **American Chamber of Commerce in Georgia (AmCham)** to connect with senior business leaders. Seek out industry-specific groups like **Startup Grind Tbilisi** to forge relevant professional connections.

**Tbilisi as a Strategic Hub:** Reframe your perspective. Tbilisi is not an isolated destination but a cost-effective base of operations for an entire region. Its strategic location and liberal visa policy make it a powerful launchpad. The significant capital you save is your strategic travel fund, allowing you to fly to Dubai for a client meeting or a conference in Berlin while maintaining a frictionless, low-cost home base.

body.content: Your Strategic Decision: Is Tbilisi the Right Base for Your Business?

The choice to establish a base in Georgia is not a lifestyle expense; it is a calculated business investment in efficiency, compliance, and growth. Tbilisi presents a unique trifecta of strategic advantages for the discerning global professional.

First is the **removal of bureaucratic friction**. For citizens of over 95 countries, Georgia offers the ability to enter, live, and work for a full year without a visa. This is a strategic asset that eliminates months of administrative anxiety and grants you immediate operational freedom.

Second is the **unmatched leverage of the 1% tax regime**. This is a powerful catalyst for capital reallocation. By registering as an Individual Entrepreneur, you reduce your tax burden on up to GEL 500,000 of annual turnover to a nominal 1%. This delta is pure growth capital—money you can reinvest in your business, deploy to enhance your quality of life, or channel into investments that secure your long-term financial autonomy.

Finally, these advantages are built upon a **high-performance, low-cost operational environment**. The delta between the cost of living in Tbilisi and other global hubs allows you to engineer an environment that minimizes personal burnout and maximizes professional output.

Ultimately, the question is not whether Tbilisi is an enjoyable place to live. The data confirms it is. The real question is whether it is a strategic platform for your global business. This playbook provides a clear, actionable framework. The opportunity in Georgia is well-documented; seizing it with confidence requires a professional approach. The path is clear.
Frequently Asked Questions

items:
**How do I register for Georgia's 1% tax for freelancers?**
The process is sequential. First, register as an **Individual Entrepreneur (IE)** at a Public Service Hall. Once complete, apply for **"Small Business Status"** through the Georgian Revenue Service's online portal (rs.ge). This status grants the 1% tax rate and becomes effective from the first day of the month *following* your application.

**What are the requirements for the Georgia Individual Entrepreneur program?**
The core requirements are: be physically present to register (or use a Power of Attorney), provide your passport, have a legal address in Georgia with owner consent, maintain an annual turnover under GEL 500,000, and engage in eligible business activities (most service-based professions like IT and marketing are eligible; legal, medical, and audit consulting are not).

**How do I open a business bank account in Georgia as a foreigner?**
After you are registered as an Individual Entrepreneur, you can open a business account. While not legally required, it is essential for clean bookkeeping. Visit a bank like TBC or Bank of Georgia and present your passport and your IE registration certificate, which contains your Tax Identification Number (TIN).

**Does Georgia's 1-year visa-free stay allow you to work?**
This is a critical distinction. The one-year visa-free policy grants you the legal right to *reside* in Georgia. To *work compliantly* as a self-employed professional and benefit from the tax system, you must formalize your activity by registering as an Individual Entrepreneur. The visa provides legal presence; the IE registration provides the legal framework for business operations.

**Is Tbilisi a good base for a US expat professional?**
Yes, for the strategic professional, Tbilisi offers a powerful combination of a low cost of living, minimal tax burden, and removal of bureaucratic hurdles. However, you remain obligated to file US taxes. Proactively managing this by leveraging tools like the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE) and filing necessary reports like the FBAR is non-negotiable.

**What are the ongoing reporting obligations for an Individual Entrepreneur in Georgia?**
Your primary duty is to file a monthly tax declaration via the Revenue Service portal (rs.ge) by the 15th of each month, reporting the previous month's gross turnover. The 1% tax is due at the same time. You must file every month, even if you have zero income.

**How does the 1% tax in Georgia interact with US tax obligations like FBAR?**
These are two separate compliance domains. The **1% Tax** is your obligation to Georgia based on your business turnover. **FBAR** is a *reporting* requirement to the U.S. Treasury if your foreign financial accounts exceed $10,000. Paying your Georgian tax does not exempt you from US filing obligations. You must manage both systems in parallel, typically using the FEIE or Foreign Tax Credit to avoid double taxation on your US return.
